text.title: STAR Question Bank by Competency
text.summary: Behavioral interview questions organized by five core competencies: leadership, problem-solving, communication, collaboration, and adaptability.

rule.statement: Use leadership questions for any role where the candidate will influence, direct, or develop others; do not assume leadership only applies to management titles.
rule.rationale: Individual contributors lead without authority (technical decisions, driving standards, unblocking teammates). Leadership questions reveal influence mechanisms, not just direct-report management.

list.item: "Tell me about a time you led a team through a significant change."
"Describe a situation where you had to motivate an underperforming team member."
"Give an example of when you made an unpopular decision and how you handled the pushback."
"Tell me about a time you delegated an important task. What did you delegate and why?"

rule.statement: Prefer problem-solving questions where the candidate had incomplete information or time pressure; easy problems with clear solutions produce undifferentiated responses.
rule.rationale: Problem-solving under ambiguity is the most differentiating signal for IC and manager roles. Questions about "a complex problem" without constraints invite retrospective polishing; adding "with incomplete information" or "under pressure" reduces the ability to construct a generic answer.

list.item: "Describe a complex problem you solved at work."
"Tell me about a time you had to make an important decision with incomplete information."
"Give an example of when you identified a problem before others on the team noticed it."
"Describe a time you had to troubleshoot a critical issue under time pressure."
"Tell me about a time you had to deliver difficult feedback or news to someone."
"Describe a situation where you had to persuade someone to change their position."
"Give an example of when you had to explain something complex to a non-technical audience."
"Tell me about a miscommunication that caused a problem. How did you resolve it?"

rule.statement: Include at least one conflict or disagreement question in every behavioral loop; candidates who only describe smooth collaboration have not been stress-tested on the most predictive dimension.
rule.rationale: How candidates describe conflict reveals their conflict resolution style, attribution tendencies (self-blame vs. other-blame), and ability to sustain relationships under stress — all strong predictors of team performance.

list.item: "Describe a successful cross-functional project you worked on. What made it succeed?"
"Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a colleague or stakeholder. How did you handle it?"
"Give an example of when you had to work with someone whose working style differed significantly from yours."
"Describe how you built a working relationship with a key stakeholder who was initially skeptical."
"Tell me about a time you had to adapt to a major change in your role, team, or company direction."
"Describe a situation where priorities suddenly shifted and you had to pivot your work."
"Give an example of when you had to learn something new quickly under pressure."
"Tell me about a time you failed at something important. What did you do, and what did you learn?"
